name: Rapid Response Playbook
description: Emergency response workflow for newly dropped critical CVEs. Designed for speed - go from CVE announcement to bug bounty submission in minimum time.
author: PocMap AI Bug Bounty Toolkit
trigger_conditions: CVE added to CISA KEV catalog
Twitter/infosec community buzzing about new exploit
Rapid increase in exploitation attempts detected (GreyNoise, etc.)
prerequisites: Pre-configured scope files for active programs
Burp Suite configured with target scope
goal: Submit bug bounty report for critical CVE within 2-6 hours of disclosure

steps.description: Get CVE details via PocMap AI
steps.action: Query AI for CVE summary, affected products, CVSS, EPSS, and exploit availability
steps.tips: This should take under 60 seconds. The AI aggregates from multiple sources instantly.

go_no_go_criteria.NO_GO: CVSS < 4.0 OR no affected products in active scope
go_no_go_criteria.CONDITIONAL: CVSS 4.0-7.0 with high EPSS - evaluate further

steps.action: Check if affected product is in any active bug bounty scope
steps.command: python -c "from pocmap.bugbounty.scope_manager import ScopeManager; sm = ScopeManager(); sm.import_scope('scope.json'); print(sm.match_cves_to_scope([cve_data]))"
steps.tips: Pre-load all scope files at toolkit init for instant matching.

steps.action: Run composite scoring to rank against other opportunities
Alert via configured webhooks about new critical CVE
steps.command: python -c "from pocmap.bugbounty.automation import NotificationManager; n = NotificationManager(); n.add_slack_webhook('{{WEBHOOK_URL}}'); n.notify_critical_cve(cve_data)"
steps.tips: Notifications keep your team synchronized and prevent duplicate work.

phases.name: Exploit Acquisition (15-45 minutes)
phases.description: Get working exploit as fast as possible

steps.action: Request PoC/exploit from AI service
steps.tips: PocMap AI may have non-public PoCs. This is your fastest source.
steps.fallback: If AI has no exploit, proceed to step 2.2 immediately

steps.action: Search GitHub for newly published exploit code
steps.tips: Sort by 'updated' to find newest repos. Check stars, forks, and recency.
quality_checks: No suspicious network calls or encoded payloads

steps.action: Check Exploit-DB for verified exploits
steps.tips: Exploit-DB verified exploits are reliable but may lag behind GitHub by hours.

steps.action: Compare available exploits and select the most reliable
security_checks: No hardcoded callbacks to unknown domains

steps.action: Spin up matching vulnerable environment in Docker
steps.tips: Have pre-built Docker images for common stacks. Use docker-compose for complex multi-service targets.
prebuilt_images: Custom images built from vendor Dockerfiles

steps.action: Execute PoC in safe mode against lab
steps.tips: Use DNS callback (interactsh.io) or sleep-based detection for safe validation. Never use destructive payloads in lab.
steps.abort_if: Exploit fails in lab - may need adaptation or target mismatch

steps.action: Carefully validate against in-scope target
rules_of_engagement: ALL traffic goes through Burp proxy
Do NOT access, modify, or delete data

steps.action: Gather proof: screenshots, requests, responses
required_evidence: Burp proxy HTTP request showing payload
Burp proxy HTTP response showing vulnerability
steps.tips: Export Burp state file. Screenshot everything. Use asciinema for terminal recordings.

phases.name: Speed Report (90-150 minutes)
phases.description: Generate and submit report as fast as possible

steps.action: Use PocMap AI to draft initial report
steps.command: python -c "from pocmap.bugbounty.automation import ReportDrafter; d = ReportDrafter(); print(d.draft_report(cve_data, 'hackerone', impact='{{IMPACT}}', steps='{{STEPS}}'))" > report.md
steps.tips: The AI-generated draft includes all CVE details. You only need to add your specific impact and steps.

steps.action: Insert screenshots, request/response pairs, and PoC code
speed_tips: Include PoC code as code block
Keep descriptions concise - triagers are busy

steps.action: Score with first.org calculator, paste result with justification
speed_tips: Use the online calculator - it's fastest
One-line justification per metric is enough for speed reports
Example: 'AV:N - Vulnerable endpoint is network-accessible without authentication'

steps.action: Final check and platform submission
Log submission ID, timestamp, and target
Calendar reminder for triage follow-up
Watch program disclosures and HackerOne Hacktivity
steps.tips: If duplicates appear, your response speed becomes important for partial credit.

steps.action: Update assessment results and scope files
steps.tips: This CVE is now 'known' - remove from active monitoring or lower priority.

speed_optimization.pre_staged_scope: Have scope.json files ready for all active programs
speed_optimization.pre_configured_webhooks: Notifications set up before CVE drops
speed_optimization.lab_templates: Docker Compose files for common stacks pre-built
speed_optimization.hotkey_shortcuts: Burp Suite project templates with target scope pre-configured
speed_optimization.report_templates: Platform-specific templates with researcher info pre-filled
speed_optimization.cve_cache: Keep local cache of recent CVE lookups for instant retrieval

risk_mitigation.speed_vs_quality: Don't sacrifice evidence quality for speed. A rejected report wastes all time.
risk_mitigation.authorization: Never test without confirmed authorization. Speed doesn't excuse policy violations.
risk_mitigation.safety: Minimal payloads only. No data access, modification, or deletion.
risk_mitigation.ethics: Report only what you can prove. Exaggeration damages credibility.
